message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: Tavily Research's report-composition architecture is a FOUR-STAGE pipeline with exactly ONE report-writing step: Planning -> WebSearch -> ResearchSubtopic (pro only) -> Generating. There is no section-by-section writer, no separate outline agent, and no self-critique/revision pass exposed anywhere.
DETAIL: Tavily's own docs enumerate every tool the agent can emit during a run. Verbatim from the streaming reference: `Planning` = 'Initializes the research plan based on the input query' (both models); `WebSearch` = 'Executes web searches to gather information' (both); `ResearchSubtopic` = 'Conducts deep research on specific subtopics' (Pro only); `Generating` = 'Generates the final research report from collected information' (both). The documented 'Research Flow Example' is: Planning tool_call/response -> WebSearch tool_call (with `queries` array) / tool_response (with `sources` array) -> (Pro mode) ResearchSubtopic tool_call/response cycles -> Generating tool_call ('Generating final report') -> Generating tool_response ('Report generated') -> Content events (streamed report chunks) -> Sources event -> Done. This identical 4-tool table is independently reproduced in Tavily's own open-source skills repo (github.com/tavily-ai/skills, skills/tavily-best-practices/references/research.md, lines 182-193), which is a second Tavily-authored source. Note: absence of a critique/revision tool is absence-of-evidence (internals are closed), but two independent Tavily-authored sources enumerate the full tool set and neither contains one.
SYSTEM: Tavily
SOURCE GIVEN: https://docs.tavily.com/documentation/api-reference/endpoint/research-streaming
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the primary source directly.

input.prompt: List verbatim every tool name documented for the research agent (e.g. Planning, WebSearch, ResearchSubtopic, Generating), their descriptions, and which model (base/pro) each applies to. Also reproduce the "Research Flow Example" sequence of events. Is there any mention of an outline agent, section-by-section writing, critique, revision, or reflection step?

input.query: Tavily Research API streaming tool_call Planning WebSearch ResearchSubtopic Generating

content.content:
# Tavily Research Agent Tools

## Tool Inventory

| Tool Name | Description | Model(s) |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Planning** | "Initializes the research plan based on the input query" | Both |
| **WebSearch** | "Executes web searches to gather information" | Both |
| **Generating** | "Generates the final research report from collected information" | Both |
| **ResearchSubtopic** | "Conducts deep research on specific subtopics" | Pro only |

## Research Flow Sequence

The documented workflow progresses through these stages:

1. Planning tool call → Initialize research strategy
2. Planning tool response → Strategy established
3. WebSearch tool call → Execute queries (includes query array)
4. WebSearch tool response → Completion with sources array
5. ResearchSubtopic cycles → Deep dives on subtopics (Pro mode)
6. Generating tool call → Compile final report
7. Generating tool response → Report compilation done
8. Content events → Report streamed in chunks
9. Sources event → Aggregated source list
10. Done event → Stream termination

## Additional Agent Capabilities

The documentation makes **no mention** of outline generation, section-by-section writing processes, critique mechanisms, revision workflows, or reflection steps in the research agent's capabilities.

Web search results for query: "Tavily Research API streaming tool_call Planning WebSearch ResearchSubtopic Generating"

Links: [{"title":"Streaming - Tavily Docs","url":"https://docs.tavily.com/documentation/api-reference/endpoint/research-streaming"},{"title":"GitHub - tavily-ai/tavily-python: The Tavily Python SDK allows for easy interaction with the Tavily API, offering the full range of our search, extract, crawl, map, and research functionalities directly from your Python programs. Easily integrate smart search, content extraction, and research capabilities into your applications, harnessing Tavily's powerful features. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/tavily-ai/tavily-python"},{"title":"Tavily API - Complete Developer Guide","url":"https://agentsapis.com/tavily-api/"},{"title":"Tavily 101: AI-powered Search for Developers | Tavily Blog","url":"https://www.tavily.com/blog/tavily-101-ai-powered-search-for-developers"},{"title":"GitHub - tavily-ai/langchain-tavily · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/tavily-ai/langchain-tavily"},{"title":"Tavily","url":"https://www.tavily.com/"},{"title":"Tavily API Platform","url":"https://app.tavily.com/home"},{"title":"Tavily Research Tool","url":"https://llamahub.ai/l/tools/llama-index-tools-tavily-research?from=tools"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Tavily Research API streaming tool_call:

## Tavily Research API Streaming

The Tavily Research API supports streaming by setting stream: true in your request, which allows you to receive research progress updates, tool calls, and final results as they're generated for a better user experience during long-running research tasks.

### Streaming Events Structure

The API responds with a text/event-stream content type, sending Server-Sent Events (SSE) as the research progresses. In Pro mode, the streaming includes ResearchSubtopic tool_call/response cycles for deeper research, generating tool_call → generating final report, generating tool_response → report generated, content events → streamed report chunks, sources event → complete list of all sources used, and a done event → stream complete.

### Key Benefits

Streaming is particularly useful for displaying research progress to users in real-time, monitoring tool calls and search queries as they execute, and receiving incremental updates during lengthy research operations.

### Python Implementation

You can create a streaming research task using the Tavily Python SDK with stream=True, then process the stream as it arrives for each chunk.

The streaming feature makes it ideal for building interactive research interfaces where users can see the research progress in real-time rather than waiting for the complete results.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

The evaluation of deepresearch APIs… | by unicodeveloper | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@unicodeveloper/ai-deepresearch-apis-in-2026-f6d89ca0c17d"},{"title":"GitHub - Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench"},{"title":"Tavily Alternatives in 2026 (After the Nebius Acquisition) | by unicodeveloper | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@unicodeveloper/tavily-alternatives-in-2026-after-the-nebius-acquisition-9de526780686"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763"},{"title":"ScaffoldAgent: Utility-Guided Dynamic Outline Optimization for Open-Ended Deep Research","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.20122"},{"title":"Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.18267"},{"title":"Deep Researcher with Sequential Plan Reflection and Candidates Crossover (Deep Researcher Reflect Evolve)","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.20843"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Tavily's DeepResearch Bench RACE leaderboard scores in 2026:

Tavily achieved 39% accuracy on the DeepResearch Bench evaluation according to a May 2026 article comparing deep research APIs. This appears to be referring to their deep research product performance.

However, for the specific RACE framework scores on the leaderboard, Tavily-research scored 52.44 overall on the RACE framework, with component scores of 52.84 (Comprehensiveness), 53.59 (Insight), 51.92 (Instruction Following), and 49.21 (Readability).

It's worth noting that Tavily Research held the state-of-the-art (SOTA) readability metric score at the time of these evaluations, though Tavily's overall performance was surpassed by systems like ADORE, which achieved a score of 52.65 on the public DeepResearch Bench leaderboard.

content.text: Both primary sources check out verbatim. Reporting.

input.reasoning:
I went to the primary source and pulled its raw markdown rather than trusting a rendered summary.

SOURCE 1 — https://docs.tavily.com/documentation/api-reference/endpoint/research-streaming (fetched live, currently online, July 2026). Section "## Tool Types" is preceded by the line "During research, you'll encounter the following tool types in streaming events:" and contains exactly four rows, verbatim:
| `Planning` | Initializes the research plan based on the input query | Both |
| `Generating` | Generates the final research report from collected information | Both |
| `WebSearch` | Executes web searches to gather information | Both |
| `ResearchSubtopic` | Conducts deep research on specific subtopics | Pro only |
The "Research Flow Example" that follows is, verbatim: 1. Planning tool_call -> 2. Planning tool_response -> 3. WebSearch tool_call (with `queries` array) -> 4. WebSearch tool_response (with `sources` array) -> 5. (Pro mode) ResearchSubtopic tool_call/response cycles -> 6. Generating tool_call ("Generating final report") -> 7. Generating tool_response ("Report generated"), followed by Content events, Sources event, Done. Every quoted string in the CLAIM's DETAIL matches the source character-for-character. Grepping the raw page for outline/critique/revise/reflect/section returns nothing.

SOURCE 2 — github.com/tavily-ai/skills, skills/tavily-best-practices/references/research.md (fetched raw from main). Lines 182-185 reproduce the same four-tool table (`Planning`/`WebSearch`/`Generating` = "mini, pro"; `ResearchSubtopic` = "pro only"), and lines 189-192 reproduce the same four-step flow. This is a genuinely independent, Tavily-authored corroboration. Two minor drifts from the claim, neither material: the table sits at 182-185 with the flow at 189-192 (claim cited "182-193," which merely brackets both), and the skills repo names the models "mini"/"pro" where the docs say "Both."

So the claim is an accurate description of what Tavily documents. Two scoping caveats the parent report must carry:

(1) The docs enumerate tool types *observable in the streaming event surface*, not Tavily's internal architecture. A section-by-section writer, outline pass, or self-critique loop could execute inside `Generating` and never emit a distinct stream event. The claim's headline sentence ("There is no section-by-section writer, no separate outline agent, and no self-critique/revision pass exposed anywhere") is only defensible with "exposed" doing load-bearing work — which the claim's own DETAIL correctly flags as absence-of-evidence. Do not let that qualifier get dropped downstream into a bare "Tavily has no revision pass."

(2) Neither source says anything whatsoever about DeepResearch Bench. This claim is purely architectural and carries no score assertion, so the 2026 leaderboard/evaluator churn (Gemini-2.5-Pro -> GPT-5.5 as RACE judge) does not touch it. But that cuts both ways: if the parent report uses this 4-stage architecture to *explain* a Tavily DRB score or ranking, that architecture-to-score causal link is a separate, unverified claim. Web search surfaced secondhand RACE numbers for Tavily (~52.44 overall, allegedly SOTA readability) from low-quality aggregator/Medium sources; I could not trace those to the official leaderboard and am explicitly NOT endorsing them.

Verdict is CONFIRMED for the claim as literally written and scoped.
input.corrected: N/A — claim confirmed as written. Two precision notes rather than corrections: (a) the skills-repo table is at lines 182-185 with the flow at 189-192, not a single 182-193 block, and it labels the models "mini, pro" rather than "Both"; (b) the docs' framing is "you'll encounter the following tool types in streaming events," which enumerates the *streamed* tool surface, not a guarantee of the full internal pipeline.
